raw.reasoning: With only 'March 4' and the anchor in 2026, the clearest default is March 4 of the current year, though another year is possible.
With the anchor at 9:00 AM on 2025-09-15, the standalone time “5pm” most clearly refers to 5:00 PM later the same day.
“The 1st” most likely refers to the next upcoming first day of a month from the anchor date, but the month is not specified and could also mean another month’s first.
Because the anchor date is Tuesday and 3pm is later the same day, the most natural reading is today at 3pm, though 'Tuesday' could sometimes refer to another Tuesday.
With no year specified, March 4 is most likely interpreted as the March 4 in the anchor year, though the next occurrence in 2027 is also plausible.
With the anchor on October 31, 'the 5th' most likely refers to the upcoming 5th of the next month, November 5, at noon, though a past or otherwise specified month is possible.
“A month ago” refers to the same day and time one calendar month before the anchor time.
“A year ago” is resolved as exactly one calendar year before the anchor time.
“A month ago” most directly means one calendar month before the anchor time, though it could also be interpreted approximately as about 30 days ago.
“In two weeks” clearly means two weeks after the anchor time, preserving the local New York clock time.
“In 6 months” is resolved by adding six calendar months to the anchor time of 2026-06-09T11:00:00 in America/New_York.
Three days before the anchor time of 2026-06-09T11:00:00-04:00 is 2026-06-06T11:00:00-04:00.
“In 3 days” clearly means three days after the anchor time, preserving the same local time.

Interpreting business days as weekdays excluding the U.S. Thanksgiving holiday on 2025-11-27 makes the third business day after the anchor Tuesday 2025-12-02, though counting only weekends would make it 2025-12-01.
“30 days ago” is a clear relative point exactly 30 days before the anchor time.
From the anchor date of Thursday 2026-03-12, the day after tomorrow is Saturday 2026-03-14.
Counting back five business days from Monday 2025-09-15, excluding the weekend, lands on Monday 2025-09-08.
“Two months from now” clearly means the same time of day two calendar months after the anchor time.
Six weeks ago is exactly 42 days before the anchor time of 2026-01-15T10:00:00-05:00.
“In three days” clearly means three days after the anchor time, preserving the same time of day.
“A month ago” most naturally means the same date and time one calendar month before the anchor, though it could loosely mean about 30 days ago.
“In 3 days” is interpreted as exactly three days after the anchor time, preserving the same local time.
“YTD” clearly means year-to-date, from the start of the current calendar year through the anchor time.
Memorial Day in the United States is the last Monday in May, which in 2026 falls on 2026-05-25.
“Labor Day” in the U.S. is the first Monday in September, and with the 2025 anchor the current-year occurrence is 2025-09-01, though without context it could also refer to another year.
With the anchor on Sunday in America/New_York, I interpret “last week” as the previous Sunday-to-Sunday calendar week, though a Monday-to-Monday ISO week is also plausible.
“This month” clearly refers to the calendar month containing the anchor date, March 2026.

With the New York locale, 'this week' most likely refers to the Sunday-start week containing the anchor date, though a Monday-start or prior-week interpretation is plausible because the anchor is Sunday.
With only “Thanksgiving” and a New York anchor date, the most likely interpretation is the next U.S. Thanksgiving, though the year is not explicitly stated.
YTD means year-to-date, from the start of the current year through the anchor time.
Interpreted “last week” as the previous Monday-through-Sunday calendar week relative to the Thursday anchor, though a Sunday-start week is also plausible.
MTD means month-to-date, so it runs from the start of the current month through the anchor time.
“This quarter” refers to the current calendar quarter containing the anchor date, which is Q1 2026 from January 1 through before April 1 in America/New_York.
The org-defined maintenance_window is the 2nd Saturday of each month from 02:00 to 06:00, and the next one on or after the anchor is March 14, 2026.
The org all_hands is the last Friday of each month from 15:00–16:00 local, and the next one after the anchor date is September 26, 2025.
Using the org calendar definition of open_enrollment as November 1–15 inclusive, the next/current-year occurrence after the anchor is in 2026, though the year was not explicitly stated.
Using the org's next_billing_cycle definition, the cycle after the current Dec 15–Jan 14 cycle runs from Jan 15 through Feb 14, represented as a half-open interval ending Feb 15.
“Q1” most likely refers to the first calendar quarter of the anchor year 2026, though the year is not explicitly stated.
“The year” most likely refers to the current year from the anchor date, so the first half is January through June 2026.
“The next three days” most likely means the 72-hour period starting now, though it could also mean the next three calendar days.
Interpreted as the first calendar week within March 2026, with a plausible alternative being a Monday-start week convention.
“Since March” most likely means from the start of March in the current year onward, though it could refer to another March if more context existed.
“This week” anchored on Thursday 2026-03-12 refers to the current calendar week, and weekdays are Monday through Friday, March 9–13, 2026.
With the anchor in June 2026, “since March” most likely means starting at the beginning of March 2026 and continuing to the present/open-ended, though a prior March is possible.
“March” most likely refers to the current March in the anchor year, 2026, and weekends are resolved as Saturday-Sunday spans, with March 1 included as a Sunday.
“The last 7 days” most commonly means the rolling seven-day period ending at the anchor time, though it could also mean the last seven calendar days.
With the anchor in March 2026, the most natural reading is March 1 through March 15 of the current year, inclusive, though the year is not explicitly stated.
With no year specified and the anchor in March 2026, this most likely refers to March 1 through March 4 of the current year, inclusive.
“June onward” most likely means starting at the beginning of June in the anchor year and continuing indefinitely, though it could also mean from now within June.
With only the month name given, the most likely choice from the June 2026 anchor is the upcoming December, though another year is possible.
“Q1” most commonly means the first calendar quarter of the anchor year, but with no year or context and the anchor near year-end, Q1 2026 or a fiscal quarter are also plausible.
With the anchor on Tuesday 2026-06-09, “this Monday” and “this Friday” clearly refer to Monday and Friday of the current week.
The months are most naturally interpreted as March and May of the anchor year 2026, though no year is explicitly stated.
Interpreted “this week” as the current Monday–Sunday week containing the anchor date, excluding Thanksgiving Day, Thursday 2025-11-27.
With the anchor in June 2026, “June 1” most likely refers to 2026-06-01, and “the week after” most likely means the following calendar week, 2026-06-08 through 2026-06-14.
With the Thursday 2026-03-12 anchor, “last week” most clearly means the previous Monday–Friday workweek, 2026-03-02 through 2026-03-06, split into 8–12 and 1–5 local time blocks.
With the anchor in 2026, “Q2 this year” is April through June 2026 and “Q2 last year” is April through June 2025.
“This month” is June 2026 from the anchor date, and “last month” is May 2026.
“Yesterday” relative to the anchor is 2026-06-08, and the two specified time windows are clear.
“This month” clearly refers to March 2026 from the anchor date, and excluding weekends leaves the weekday spans in that month.
Interpreted “past month” as the rolling month ending at the anchor time, filtered to Tuesday through Thursday 8am–12pm, with today’s interval ending at now.
Interpreted “the last 30 days” as the rolling 30-day window ending at the anchor time, restricted to Monday–Friday business days and clipped at the window boundaries.
The word “current” refers to the anchor time, which is 2025-12-30T13:00:00-05:00.
“Latest” indicates the most recent report available as of the anchor time, but no specific report date or reporting period is given.
With the anchor on Tuesday, “the weekend” most likely refers to the upcoming Saturday–Sunday weekend, though the previous weekend is also plausible.
“Next month” from the March 12, 2026 anchor is April 2026, and “early” most commonly means roughly the first ten days, though the exact cutoff is subjective.
“The last few months” most likely means a rolling period of about three months ending now, but “few” and whether to use calendar months are both ambiguous.
“Recently” indicates a past period up to now but gives no precise duration, so I used the last 7 days as a concrete default.
“This afternoon” most likely refers to the afternoon of the anchor date, conventionally interpreted as noon to early evening, though exact afternoon boundaries can vary.
“Soon” indicates an unspecified near-future period starting from the anchor time, but it gives no concrete endpoint or duration.
With the anchor on 2025-12-30, “the new year” most likely refers to the upcoming calendar year 2026, though it could also mean New Year’s Day specifically.
“End of year” most commonly refers to the last day of the current year, 2025, though it can sometimes mean a broader late-December period.
“Around the holidays” is vague, but in the U.S. near the anchor date it most likely refers to the holiday season from Thanksgiving through New Year’s Day.
“Mid-month” most likely refers to the middle of the current month relative to the anchor, conventionally June 15, 2026, though it could also mean a broader mid-month period.
“About a month ago” most likely refers to roughly one month before the anchor date, which is 2026-02-12, though the approximation could allow nearby dates.

With the anchor in March 2026, “over the summer” most likely refers to the upcoming summer season, interpreted as meteorological summer June through August, though astronomical summer is also plausible.

With the anchor on the Wednesday before U.S. Thanksgiving, “the long weekend” most likely refers to the Thanksgiving long weekend starting Thursday, November 27, 2025, though it could also be interpreted as starting Friday for some people.
From Tuesday 2026-06-09, “next Friday” most likely refers to the upcoming Friday, 2026-06-12, though some usage could mean the Friday after that.
